item.title: Paraguay declares national holiday after team shock Germany at World Cup
item.description: Paraguay President Santiago Pena declared a national holiday for Tuesday to celebrate the country’s shock victory over Germany that sent them into the World Cup round of 16. The South American side stunned four-time world champions Germany 4-3 on penalties after the teams were tied 1-1 following extra time on Monday, pulling off one of the biggest upsets in World Cup history.
item.content:encoded:
Paraguay President Santiago Pena declared a national holiday for Tuesday to celebrate the country’s shock victory over Germany that sent them into the World Cup round of 16.

The South American side stunned four-time world champions Germany 4-3 on penalties after the teams were tied 1-1 following extra time on Monday, pulling off one of the biggest upsets in World Cup history.

“Today, an entire country celebrates,” Pena posted on X along with a picture of himself signing the decree.

“It celebrates the victory of a team that represents the deepest part of our identity: the grit, the faith, and the strength of people who never give up.”

The text of the decree, also shared by Pena on social media, said that Paraguay’s victory went far beyond sport and justified a nationwide celebration.

“The government cannot remain indifferent to this tremendous achievement,” the decree read. “It is necessary to facilitate the gathering of all Paraguayans in celebration of this historic day.”

Paraguay is the second South American country to declare a holiday after a surprise World Cup result against Germany at the tournament.

Last week, Ecuador President Daniel Noboa also announced a national holiday after his country secured a 2-1 victory over the Germans in Group E to reach the knockout stage.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Mr Sam’s genesis | Kurukitoga returns to rugby league after pivot to music due to career-altering injury
item.description: TO many Fijians, he is simply “MR.SAM” – the singer whose music has won fans across the country. Long before he became a familiar name in Fiji’s music industry, Sam Kurukitoga was chasing a different dream in the game of rugby league.
item.content:encoded:
TO many Fijians, he is simply “MR.SAM” – the singer whose music has won fans across the country.

Long before he became a familiar name in Fiji’s music industry, Sam Kurukitoga was chasing a different dream in the game of rugby league.

Now, after two years away from the game because of a career-threatening injury, the Nadera Panthers fullback is back where it all began, determined to prove that his passion for rugby league never disappeared.

Kurukitoga made his long-awaited return in the Vodafone Cup this season after recovering from a broken arm that sidelined him for two years.

While the injury forced him off the field, he unexpectedly launched a successful music career that has since made him one of Fiji’s recognised emerging local artists.

Despite his success as a musician, the 30-year-old said rugby league has always remained close to his heart.

“I was out for two years due to injury. I broke my arm, this is my comeback,” Kurukitoga said.

He hails from Naloto, Verata, in the province of Tailevu and Kurukitoga grew up in a family deeply connected to rugby league.

He began playing in year seven with Panther’s junior side before progressing through the club’s age teams then the senior squad.

Today, he finds himself sharing the field with teammates he watched while growing up.

“I’ve always loved rugby league,” he said.

“My uncle inspired me very much. Watching him play while I was growing up, made me want to play too

“Most of my uncles played for this team, and that’s what made me pursue the sport.”

His rugby career appeared to be gaining momentum before disaster struck.

Only days after being named in the Fiji Residents squad, Kurukitoga’s arm was broken and it effectively ruled him out of the game for two years.

For Kurukitoga, suffering a career-altering injury at the cusp of what would have been a moment of great success was something he found really difficult to accept, understand and handle.

“It was heartbreaking because I knew my name was there. Then I had to watch my teammates represent Fiji while I was recovering.”

Doctors advised him to spend three years away for rehabilitating the injury, but after two years he was ready to make his return.

During that difficult period, Kurukitoga discovered another talent that would change his life – music.

“My singing career began when I broke my arm.”

Unable to play rugby, he turned to music as a way of coping with the disappointment.

What started as a past time soon developed into a professional career, earning him recognition throughout Fiji under the name “MR.SAM”.

His stage name became the latest sensation hitting Fijian airwaves with his viral hit ‘JEKE TANIELA’, released last year.

He has also released songs such as “Vuavuai”, “Kua ni Dro”, “Tadra Ga”, “Love at First Sight”, “Kutu”, “Angel Baby”, “Niko Yali”, “Tu Mai Vei”, “Voli Gauna”, “I Tima”, “Ravi Yani” and “Naica Li.”

Collaborations with Fijian rappers JU BEN and Red Child have also added to his developing music catalog.

JU BEN is also one of the nation’s emerging musical acts, whose famously collaborated with singer Liz Vamarasi on the 2025 hit “Vakadua.”

Red Child is a rapper whose song “Gone Sisi”, released in 2008 became an instant hit among listeners.

Today, he balances rugby league, a full-time job and a growing music career.

“It gets hectic and time-consuming, but I’m trying to balance everything.”

Although music has brought him success, Kurukitoga admitted the desire to pick up what he left behind in rugby league never faded.

item.title: Amateur golf unfolds in style
item.description: GOLFERS competed for a variety of prizes in the Eastern Amateur Championships at the Fiji Golf Club in Suva over the weekend. Ishaan Shah won the overall gross of the A Grade with a score of 208 points, while Shakeel Pillay was placed second with a score of 218 and Jainendra P as second runner-up with 225 points.
item.content:encoded:
GOLFERS competed for a variety of prizes in the Eastern Amateur Championships at the Fiji Golf Club in Suva over the weekend.

Ishaan Shah won the overall gross of the A Grade with a score of 208 points, while Shakeel Pillay was placed second with a score of 218 and Jainendra P as second runner-up with 225 points.

Winners were presented prizes such as home appliances, accessories and power tools.

Prize recipients: A Grade – Overall Gross: Ishaan Shah, 208 points. 1st runner-up gross: Shakeel Pillay, 218, 2nd runner-up gross: Jainendra P, 225, 3rd runner-up gross: Nitulesh Mali, 231. Overall net winner: Ravinesh Gounden, 210. 1st runner-up net: Seresio C, 211, 2nd runner-up net: William D, 211 on countback, 3rd runner-up net: Tikiko Temo, 214. Day one net: David Qiolevu with 108.5 points, day two gross: Shalen Prasad, 116, day two net: Mohammed Rizwan, 107, day two gross: Ryan Kumar, 113.

B Grade – Overall Gross: David Whitehead with 173 points, overall net: Praneel C, 143. 1st runner-up net: Jerome Kedo, 145, 2nd runner-up net: Mohammed Ishak, 148, 3rd runner-up net: Gyanesh Reuben, 150 on countback. Day 1 net: Feizal Haniff, 68 points. Day 1 gross: Virendra P, 81, day 2 net: Matt Cauty, 73, day 2 gross: Fenton L, 87 points.

C Grade – Overall Gross: Ashwin Nand, 174 points, overall net: Hamzah H, 135. 1st runner-up net: Anil Prasad, 136, 2nd runner-up net: Ryan Tierney, 139 on countback, 3rd runner-up net: Patrick Nadan, 139 on countback. Day 1 net: Rajeev Patel, 65 points, day 1 gross: Asif Hussein, 90, day 2 net: Sunil, 71, day 2 gross: Jone Mua, 86 points.

Ladies – Overall Gross: Raina Kumar, 145 points. Overall Net: Kamini Singh, 142. 1st runner-up net: Lillian Allen, 148, 2nd runner-up net: Margie Browne, 151, 3rd runner-up net: Lusi Leqa, 152 on countback. Day 1 net: Lynn Natividad, 70, day 1 gross: Natalia, 89. Day 2 net: Agnes Fong Toy 77, day 2 gross: Sunita K, 93 points.

Novelties – Day 1 long drive (L/D) ladies: Lusi Leqa, day 1 L/D men: Ishaan Shah. Day 1 nearest-to-the-pin (NTP) Hole 2: Michael Nacola, day 1 NTP Hole 5: Ravinesh C, day 1 NTP Hole 12: Seresio, day 1 NTP Hole 16: Asif Hussain. Day 2 L/D ladies: Raina Kumar, day 2 L/D men: Ishaan Shah, day 2 NTP Hole 2: Lusi Leqa, day 2 NTP Hole 5: Margie Brown, day 2 NTP Hole 12: Ethan Qiolevu, day 2 NTP Hole 16: Temo Tikiko.

Juniors – Overall Gross (10+) – Isikeli P, 192 points. Overall Net (10+) – Nathan B, 125 points. Runner-up Net (10+) – Rusiate B – 148 points on countback, 1st Net – Mohammed Faiyaz – 151 on countback, 2nd Net – Ethan Q, 151 on countback, 3rd Net – Deborah – 161 points.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Four test positive in drug tests
item.description: FIJI Football Association chief executive officer Mohammed Yusuf has confirmed that only four players returned positive results during recent drug testing conducted during competitions. Mr Mohammed said the association carried out routine testing and found no major issues apart from the few cases recorded.
item.content:encoded:
FIJI Football Association chief executive officer Mohammed Yusuf has confirmed that only four players returned positive results during recent drug testing conducted during competitions.

Mr Mohammed said the association carried out routine testing and found no major issues apart from the few cases recorded.

‘We also conducted drug testing. There were no issues, only four cases last week, but in the senior division we found one positive case. We will deal with it,” he said.

He did not provide further details on the individuals involved but confirmed that the matter will be handled according to the association’s disciplinary processes.

He said the Fiji Football Association would now shift focus to its next major tournament, the Battle of the Giants, which will be held in Ba in September.

“We will see everyone back in Ba in September for the Battle of the Giants,” he said.

When asked about the possibility of bringing more tournaments back to Labasa, he said they remains committed to spreading competitions across the country.

“Sure, we are indebted to the people at all levels, they really support football, and Fiji Football will come back. Definitely.”

He said the strong support from northern fans continues to make Labasa a key venue for football events in Fiji.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Raibevu earns a spot in the Fijian Drua
item.description: QUEEN Victoria School fly half and sprint champion Jone Raibevu has taken another major step towards professional rugby after earning a place in the Fijian Drua development squad through the club’s grassroots talent pathway. The Kalabu, Naitasiri youngster was identified during the Drua foundation’s talent scouting program in 2024 and has since progressed through the club’s junior development syst
item.content:encoded:
QUEEN Victoria School fly half and sprint champion Jone Raibevu has taken another major step towards professional rugby after earning a place in the Fijian Drua development squad through the club’s grassroots talent pathway.

The Kalabu, Naitasiri youngster was identified during the Drua foundation’s talent scouting program in 2024 and has since progressed through the club’s junior development system.

Raibevu said the opportunity came as a surprise.

“I was really surprised when I got the call about the opportunity. I was also excited when the Drua committee told me that I’ll be joining the Drua development next season,” Raibevu said.

The teenager first caught the attention of Drua scouts during the 2024 Fiji Secondary Schools Rugby Eastern Zone competition before being selected into the inaugural Fijian Drua junior development programme.

One of Raibevu’s most memorable and impressive performance came while representing Queen Victoria School when, at just 16-years-old, he successfully slotted a remarkable 57m penalty kick, in the final of the Eastern Zone Vodafone Deans Trophy competition.

The long-range effort highlighted not only the fly-half’s powerful boot but also the composure and confidence that have made him one of Fiji’s most promising emerging playmakers.

Away from rugby, Raibevu has also excelled in athletics. The Queen Victoria School student won the prestigious blue-ribbon event at the Tailevu Zone athletics competition.

Fijian Drua chief executive Jeff Miller said Raibevu’s rise reflected the success of the club’s development pathway.

“Jone’s signing is another great example of what can be achieved when the Fiji Rugby Union and the Drua invest in identifying and developing young Fijian talent at an early age,” Miller said.

The Drua Foundation was established to strengthen rugby development throughput Fiji by identifying promising young players, providing access to quality coaching and helping remove barriers for talented athletes from communities across the country.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Countdown | Clock ticks to Flying Fijians opener in Wales
item.description: THIS Sunday in Wales, the FIJI Water Flying Fijians run out for their first Nations Championship match against the hosts in Cardiff. For the past seven months, the Fiji Rugby Union has set the plans and moved the pieces to align itself with seeing the team take a front row seat amongst the biggest teams in the world as a tier one side.
item.content:encoded:
THIS Sunday in Wales, the FIJI Water Flying Fijians run out for their first Nations Championship match against the hosts in Cardiff.

For the past seven months, the Fiji Rugby Union has set the plans and moved the pieces to align itself with seeing the team take a front row seat amongst the biggest teams in the world as a tier one side.

And once the Flying Fijians run onto the field at the Cardiff City Stadium, they move the wheels of change in motion once the whistle blows for kick-off.

One person who has his own front row seat to the action will be the interim head coach himself, Senirusi Seruvakula.

Seruvakula’s grip on the coaching baton comes at a time when all eyes will fall on his ability to take the team to new heights in the championship with a seasoned experience built upon years of standing in the wings and waiting to be called forward to take up this role.

And for any player who wishes to march to the beat of a different tune to the one he has taught the squad, the crux of his words regarding it is simple – get out!

Our nation’s final squad for this campaign will be led by captain Tevita Ikanivere, who flew in from Japan after his first Japan Rugby League One season with the Mie Honda Heat and vice-captained by Frank Lomani and Temo Mayanavanua, both donning the white jumper after a long season with the Swire Shipping Fijian Drua.

The Flying Fijians have been preparing in Wales since their arrival last week, after extended long-haul travel in the air from Nadi to Hong Kong, and then to Heathrow International Airport in London, England.

Since their arrival in Cardiff, the team has had the opportunity to meet local leaders and also meet the Fijian community there.

Far from the familiar sandy shores and the sounds of home, the team is backed by a large Fijian community in the United Kingdom mostly made up of those working in the British armed forces and others employed in different sectors of work.

Their support will be the team’s 16th man and coupled with the tireless efforts of the FRU’s marketing team to drive in new fans and build a strong European fanbase for the Flying Fijians, it sets the stage for a opening fixture too hard to miss.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Olympic Day shines spotlight on Fiji’s minor sports
item.description: THE Olympic Day celebrations have provided a platform for Fiji’s lesser-known sports to inspire the next generation of athletes. The annual event was held at Albert Park in Suva on Saturday.
item.content:encoded:
THE Olympic Day celebrations have provided a platform for Fiji’s lesser-known sports to inspire the next generation of athletes.

The annual event was held at Albert Park in Suva on Saturday.

Speaking to Times Sport, FASANOC chief executive officer Vanessa Kilner said the annual event brought together 10 national sporting federations, giving members of the public the chance to try a variety of Olympic sports.

The day began with a fitness boot camp led by the Australian Defence Force before participants enjoyed a healthy breakfast of Weet-Bix and fresh fruit, followed by opportunities to try sports including gymnastics, baseball, flag football, taekwondo, judo, archery, volleyball, para-shotput, badminton and hockey.

Kilner said the event was particularly important in a country where rugby dominates the sporting landscape.

“Being a rugby nation, it’s important to showcase our minor sports,” she said.

“Many of these sports have actually been to the Olympic Games, so today creates awareness and encourages people to come down and have a go.”

This year’s Olympic Day theme, “Let’s Move – You Can Do This,” was designed to encourage people of all ages and abilities to become active without fear of failure.

“A lot of people think, ‘I don’t know if I’m going to fail’ or ‘I’m not good enough’. The message is simple – you can do this,” Kilner said.

She said the participating national federations were at the heart of grassroots development, using the event as a pathway to attract new participants into their clubs.

Kilner also acknowledged the support of several organisations that helped stage the event, including the Australian High Commission, the Australian Defence Force, Legends Sportswear, CJ Patel and the United States Embassy.

She said the American Embassy’s involvement was also helping promote flag football and baseball while building excitement towards the 2028 Los Angeles Olympic Games.

Although the next Olympics are still two years away, Kilner said Fiji’s elite athletes were already on the pathway.

“The next Olympics are in Los Angeles in 2028. This year we have the Commonwealth Games, then next year the Pacific Games, and it’s all part of that four-year journey that culminates in the Olympic Games,” she said.

She said the Olympic Day was also about inspiring more Fijians to embrace sportand the dream of one day representing the nation on the world’s biggest sporting stage.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Ba reigns | 51-year wait ends in Labasa
item.description: Despite finishing second runner-up in the pool stages, Ba completed a remarkable turnaround to win the Fiji FACT title after defeating Navua 3-1 in a dramatic penalty shootout at Subrail Park yesterday. The final remained goalless after 90 minutes and extra time, forcing the match to be decided from the spot.
item.content:encoded:
Despite finishing second runner-up in the pool stages, Ba completed a remarkable turnaround to win the Fiji FACT title after defeating Navua 3-1 in a dramatic penalty shootout at Subrail Park yesterday.

The final remained goalless after 90 minutes and extra time, forcing the match to be decided from the spot.

Navua missed three penalties while Ba missed one, as the Men in Black held their nerve to seal the title.

The victory ends a 16-year drought for Ba in the Fiji FACT, with their last title coming in 2010.

It also delivers a historic breakthrough at Subrail Park, ending a 51 year wait for a trophy at the venue, the last coming in 1975.

Ba coach Ravinesh Kumar described the win as a proud and emotional moment for the club and its supporters.

“I feel very happy for the people of Ba and the Ba fans. Ba has won a trophy at Subrail Park after 51 years, so this is a big moment in history,” he said.

Singh said a win in Labasa has been long overdue.

“The last time Ba won here was in 1975, and now, after 51 years, Ba has finally won this trophy at Subrail Park.

“The last time Ba won the Fiji FACT was in 2010, that’s 16 years ago.”

He said both sides deserved credit for a tough final.

“I would say the performance of the boys must be applauded.”

He said that Navua also played very well adding penalties was anyone’s game. Ba received $17,000 for the win while Navua walked away $5000 richer.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
